title: Internal: Metering sanity checks kickoff (Finance + Eng + Product)
preview: Kickoff meeting to align on the "meter sanity" initiative: define what we will monitor (freshness, drift vs ledger, duplicates/idempotency, null dims like region/model, cache-hit attribution), agree on owners and escalation, and set a 6–8 week timeline with a shadow-billing compare window. Finance called out month-end close constraints (usage freeze window, no retro invoice changes without sign-off). Engineering outlined current failure modes (double-counting on retries/fallback, embeddings batching accounting, late-arriving events) and proposed automated checks + dashboards with paging thresholds.

title: Northwind Labs POC weekly sync - meters + usage dashboards
preview:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Meeting: Northwind Labs POC weekly sync - meters + usage dashboards
Date: 2026-02-14
Time: 9:02 AM PT
Duration: 35 minutes

Attendees
- Jordan Blake (Redwood Inference)
- Logan Wright (Redwood Inference)
- Nadia Rahman (Redwood Inference)
- Priya Desai (Northwind Labs)
- Ben Hartman (Northwind Labs)
- Sarah Kim (Northwind Labs)

Summary (auto-generated)
Northwind reviewed Redwood’s usage dashboard and asked for clarity on token counting, especially when cache hits occur and when requests are streamed. Redwood explained the difference between raw tokens and billed tokens, and how cache-hit attribution is represented in new usage API fields. The group discussed reconciliation between console numbers, usage exports, and invoice-like totals, plus minor concerns about timezone alignment for daily exports.

Topics
- Console usage dashboard filters (model, region, endpoint)
- Cache-hit attribution and how it affects billed tokens
- New usage API fields (billed_tokens vs raw_tokens, cache_hit indicators)
- Streaming semantics and partial completion behavior
- Reconciliation workflow and who should use which numbers

Questions (auto-generated)
- When prefix caching triggers, are “saved” tokens explicitly visible in the export?
- Are cached prompts billed at zero or reduced rate, and does that show up as billed_tokens?
- How are retries / fallbacks represented (one request_id or multiple)?
- Can we get daily exports aligned to a customer timezone?

Transcript

[00:00] Jordan Blake: Alright, cool. We’re live. Thanks everyone for hopping on. This is the Northwind POC weekly check-in, focus today on meters, the usage dashboard, and the cache hit stuff that came up in Slack.

[00:10] Priya Desai: Yep, thanks Jordan. We pulled the dashboard yesterday and… it’s good, but we have questions.

[00:16] Ben Hartman: Mostly around “why did billed tokens go down” on some days where our traffic was up.

[00:21] Jordan Blake: Love that problem to have. Logan and Nadia are here, they own a lot of the telemetry definitions. Sarah, good to meet you.

[00:27] Sarah Kim: Hi. I’m here because the numbers will end up in a spreadsheet on my side, so I need to understand what we’re looking at.

[00:34] Jordan Blake: Totally. Quick agenda: (1) walk the dashboard view you’re using, (2) talk cache-hit attribution and new usage API fields, (3) reconcile how you should interpret daily totals, and then next steps.

[00:49] Nadia Rahman: Sounds good.

[00:51] Logan Wright: Yep.

[00:53] Jordan Blake: Priya, do you want to share what you saw? Like which chart or which tab.

[00:58] Priya Desai: Sure. We’re in the Console, Usage tab, then “Hosted API”. We filtered to the llama model—uh, sorry, “Redwood Verified Llama 3.1 70B” I think. And region us-west.

[01:12] Ben Hartman: The panel shows Requests, Prompt tokens, Completion tokens, and then there’s “Billed tokens” now, which I don’t think was there last week.

[01:21] Jordan Blake: Yep, we added “billed tokens” as a first-class line item. It used to be implied.

[01:26] Priya Desai: Right. So the day we turned on caching on our gateway, we expected prompt tokens to go down, and they did. But we also see billed tokens drop more than prompt tokens, which is confusing.

[01:39] Logan Wright: Got it. So there are two things in play. One is your own caching, like if you’re caching upstream and not sending requests, then requests and tokens go down. The other is Redwood-side prefix/KV caching, which can still happen even if you’re still sending requests. In that case, you might send the same prefix and we can reuse parts of the compute path.

[01:57] Ben Hartman: But does that mean you bill fewer tokens? Or you bill the same tokens but just cheaper on your margin.

[02:04] Nadia Rahman: It depends on the product policy. For the POC you’re on, we’re tracking both raw_tokens and billed_tokens. Raw tokens are literally what the model processes in the tokenization sense. Billed tokens are the tokens that are eligible for billing after we apply rules like cache hit attribution, retries de-dupe, and certain streaming edge cases.

[02:25] Sarah Kim: Sorry—so billed tokens can be less than raw tokens even for the same request.

[02:29] Nadia Rahman: Yes. Example: if we have a cache hit on the prompt prefix, we can attribute some prompt tokens as cache_hit tokens. Depending on plan configuration, those may be billed at zero or at a reduced rate. Internally it’s represented as billed_tokens_prompt and billed_tokens_completion. In the UI we’re collapsing it into one “billed tokens” for now.

[02:49] Priya Desai: That’s the key thing. We need to know what is “saved” and how you represent it.

[02:54] Logan Wright: The new usage export fields include cache_hit boolean and cache_hit_tokens (or “cache_tokens_saved” depending on the endpoint). I’m going to say this carefully because naming changed once: we have raw_prompt_tokens, raw_completion_tokens, billed_prompt_tokens, billed_completion_tokens, and then cache_hit_prompt_tokens as a separate dimension.

[03:17] Ben Hartman: Wait, so it’s not actually “saved tokens” it’s “prompt tokens that were served from cache” basically.

[03:22] Logan Wright: Right. It’s attribution, not a claim that no compute happened at all, but in the common case it correlates with reduced compute.

[03:29] Priya Desai: Okay. On our side we’re doing deterministic prompt templates, so it should be pretty high hit rate.

[03:36] Jordan Blake: That’s consistent with what we saw; your workload is very cache friendly.

[03:40] Sarah Kim: So for finance, the number I should rely on is billed tokens, not prompt+completion.

[03:46] Jordan Blake: Correct. For forecasting and invoice alignment, billed tokens is the one.

[03:51] Ben Hartman: But in the UI, sometimes prompt+completion is higher, sometimes lower. I saw one hour where completion tokens were zero but the request succeeded.

[04:01] Logan Wright: That can happen on streaming if the client disconnects early. The model may have generated some tokens, but if the stream is cut at the transport layer, the event we get might mark the completion as partial. We should still have billed_completion_tokens if we emitted them, but there are edge cases where the final chunk isn’t recorded.

[04:20] Priya Desai: That’s the “partial chunk” thing we saw. Like we had a 200 response but our app timed out waiting for the last bytes.

[04:28] Nadia Rahman: Yep. There is also the retry/fallback path. If we retry server-side, we have to make sure we don’t double count. We’ve been hardening idempotency keys on usage events so the request_id is the unit of billing even if there were internal attempts.

[04:45] Ben Hartman: Good. Because we were worried about double charging. We saw that in another vendor.

[04:50] Jordan Blake: Totally fair. That’s one of the reasons we’re adding these fields explicitly and doing “shadow billing compare” before any meter changes affect invoices. For your POC, you’re basically getting the newest semantics.

[05:03] Sarah Kim: I’d like to understand what I can reconcile. Like if I pull daily usage exports, will it match what the UI shows.

[05:11] Logan Wright: It should within a small lag. UI is near-real-time aggregated. Exports are usually hourly to daily and can have late-arriving events. We typically recommend a freshness window of, say, 24 hours before you treat a day as final.

[05:27] Sarah Kim: That’s important for month end.

[05:30] Jordan Blake: We can talk about freeze windows later, but yes, we can do a “close” process.

[05:36] Priya Desai: On the cache hit attribution, does it apply to completion tokens too?

[05:41] Nadia Rahman: Generally cache hits apply to prompt tokens—prefix caching. For completions, we don’t “cache” generation the same way, unless you do your own application layer caching of outputs. So you’ll mostly see cache_hit_prompt_tokens and raw/billed completion tokens unchanged.

[06:00] Ben Hartman: So if billed tokens drop a lot, it’s mostly prompt side.

[06:03] Nadia Rahman: Correct.

[06:05] Priya Desai: One more nuance: we use tool calls sometimes. Does the tool call tokens show up in completion or prompt.

[06:12] Logan Wright: Great question. Tool/function call tokens typically land in completion tokens because they’re generated by the model, but depending on the API shape they can be separated. In the OpenAI compatible endpoints, it gets weird because the tool call is in the assistant message but it’s structured.

[06:29] Nadia Rahman: We normalize it as “completion” for token accounting, and then the usage event includes a flag like tool_call_present. Not sure the UI shows that yet.

[06:41] Priya Desai: Okay, fine.

[06:43] Jordan Blake: Let’s anchor on what you need for the POC decision. You want predictable spend and a way to explain to your leadership that caching is real and not just Redwood changing numbers.

[06:54] Priya Desai: Yes.

[06:55] Sarah Kim: And we need a daily report that we can compare to our internal counts.

[07:01] Ben Hartman: And also when we switch models, the tokens might change because tokenization differs.

[07:07] Nadia Rahman: Yep. Tokenization can vary by model family and tokenizer version. We use the tokenizer associated with the deployed model build. That’s why reconciling “our” tokens to “your” tokens only works if you use the same tokenizer implementation.

[07:24] Priya Desai: We do use a local tokenizer, but it’s probably not exactly the same.

[07:29] Logan Wright: That’s common. The best recon is request_id level: compare the usage export for a few known prompts and completions. If you can provide 2-3 request_ids where you see a difference, we can show you the raw_prompt_tokens/raw_completion_tokens from our side.

[07:47] Priya Desai: Okay, I can do that.

[07:50] Jordan Blake: Can we pivot to the dashboard for a second. Ben, you mentioned there’s a “cache hit rate” panel too?

[07:56] Ben Hartman: Yeah. It’s on the right, “Cache hit %” and “Cached prompt tokens”. It’s not super clear if that’s a rate of requests or tokens.

[08:06] Logan Wright: That’s a good callout. In v1, it’s token-based: cached prompt tokens divided by total prompt tokens. We’re adding a request-based hit rate as a separate metric, because it’s easier to reason about sometimes.

[08:22] Nadia Rahman: Also, the definition of “cache hit” can be partial. If the first 200 tokens hit but the rest is new, it still counts as a cache hit, but not 100% cached.

[08:33] Ben Hartman: That might be what we’re seeing, because our templates share the system prompt and first few messages, but the last message varies.

[08:42] Priya Desai: Exactly.

[08:44] Jordan Blake: So on the business side, you should see billed prompt tokens reduce proportional to cached prompt tokens. We can validate with a sample export.

[08:55] Sarah Kim: That’s what I want. Like, “show me a CSV with columns and I can do the math.”

[09:00] Jordan Blake: Great. We’ll do that.

[09:03] Priya Desai: One more thing. In the usage export we pulled last week, there was no cache field. Is that just not enabled.

[09:11] Logan Wright: Yeah. Those fields were behind a feature gate. We can enable it for your org. That will add columns; it won’t break existing parsing unless you’re strict about schema.

[09:24] Ben Hartman: We are strict.

[09:26] Jordan Blake: Okay, then we should version it. We can share a “v2 export schema” and you can switch when ready. In the API it’s additive fields, so older clients ignore them. But if you’re doing CSV ingestion with exact headers, we’ll coordinate.

[09:42] Sarah Kim: Please coordinate.

[09:45] Nadia Rahman: Also, there’s a distinction: usage API vs invoice. For POC you’re not getting invoices, but we’re aligning the usage API with what would be invoiced, meaning billed_tokens is what would map to charges.

[10:01] Priya Desai: Great.

[10:03] Ben Hartman: Can you confirm how region impacts pricing. Like if the dashboard says us-east sometimes, does that mean we got routed.

[10:11] Logan Wright: There is smart routing on capacity, but for POC we mostly keep you pinned to us-west unless we’re failing over. If you see another region, it might be either a fallback or a mis-tagged region dimension—there was a bug we fixed recently. But for your account, I can check.

[10:29] Jordan Blake: Logan, can you pull a seven-day report by region for them.

[10:33] Logan Wright: Yep.

[10:34] Priya Desai: We did see some “unknown” region values in export.

[10:38] Nadia Rahman: That’s exactly the missing dimension issue. It should be resolved, but there might be old events still.

[10:45] Sarah Kim: Old events as in last week.

[10:48] Logan Wright: Yeah, within the last couple weeks. We can backfill, but for your POC, we can just annotate it.

[10:56] Jordan Blake: Cool. Let’s talk about what you need to present internally. Priya, is it more performance or cost.

[11:03] Priya Desai: Both, but honestly the CFO question is “why is this line item unpredictable.” So the metering clarity is huge.

[11:12] Sarah Kim: Yes.

[11:13] Ben Hartman: We can handle the tokenization variance, but we need to understand what Redwood counts.

[11:19] Nadia Rahman: Then we should share a short dictionary:
- raw_prompt_tokens: tokenizer count for prompt
- raw_completion_tokens: tokenizer count for generated output
- billed_prompt_tokens: raw prompt tokens minus any cache-attributed tokens (or discounted, depending)
- billed_completion_tokens: typically equals raw completion tokens, minus de-duped retries
- cache_hit_prompt_tokens: portion of prompt tokens served from cache
- cache_hit: boolean if any portion of prompt was cache hit

[11:47] Priya Desai: Perfect. That’s what we need.

[11:50] Jordan Blake: Nadia, can you send that as an email after.

[11:53] Nadia Rahman: Yep.

[11:54] Ben Hartman: There’s still a question: do you ever bill for tokens generated during a server retry. Like if you generate 200 tokens, fail, retry, generate again.

[12:05] Logan Wright: We bill the successful request once, with de-dupe logic so internal attempts do not show up as multiple billed events. In the raw events, we may record attempts for debugging, but billing uses a stable idempotency key.

[12:20] Nadia Rahman: And for streaming, if the client disconnects, we’re working on making it consistent: do you bill only what was delivered, or what was generated. Current policy is bill what was delivered/confirmed, but it’s an area we’re tightening. That’s why you might see completion tokens 0 in some rare cases; we’d rather undercount than double count.

[12:40] Sarah Kim: Understood. That’s actually good.

[12:43] Priya Desai: We prefer that. The worst thing is arguing about phantom tokens.

[12:47] Jordan Blake: Exactly.

[12:49] Ben Hartman: Another small thing: the dashboard shows “Requests” but we have batched requests for embeddings in other systems. Not sure if that’s relevant here.

[12:58] Logan Wright: For chat/completions it’s 1 request equals 1 API call. For embeddings, if you send an array of inputs, we count it as 1 request but multiple “units” for embeddings. For your current POC you’re mostly chat, so not relevant.

[13:14] Priya Desai: Good.

[13:15] Jordan Blake: Let’s do a quick live readback. Ben, what’s the main concern you want closed by next week.

[13:21] Ben Hartman: I want a clear mapping between cache hit metrics and billed token reductions. If we can show one day’s worth of exports and compute it, we’re good.

[13:32] Priya Desai: And I want to confirm our internal “token per request” metrics aren’t wildly off because of tokenizer differences.

[13:40] Sarah Kim: And I want timezone alignment. The export day boundary is currently UTC, right.

[13:47] Logan Wright: Yes, it’s UTC by default.

[13:49] Sarah Kim: We operate on Pacific time. Otherwise daily charts look spiky.

[13:54] Jordan Blake: We’ve heard that before. We can either (a) provide timezone param in export, or (b) provide hourly exports and you roll up. Right now, easiest is hourly plus your rollup.

[14:09] Sarah Kim: Hourly is fine as long as it’s stable.

[14:12] Logan Wright: It’s stable, but there is still late arrival. We’ll note the freshness SLA.

[14:18] Nadia Rahman: And for month-end we can establish a “freeze” where after a certain time we stop backfilling without explicit approval. That’s more for paid contracts though.

[14:29] Sarah Kim: Good to know.

[14:31] Jordan Blake: We’ll keep it simple for the POC.

[14:34] Priya Desai: One more: are you planning to change these meter definitions soon. We don’t want the numbers shifting mid-test.

[14:43] Jordan Blake: Great question. The definitions are stabilizing. We’re adding fields, but we’re not changing how billed tokens are computed for you without notice. If there’s a meter fix, we’d run it in shadow mode first and tell you. Logan/Nadia can confirm.

[14:58] Logan Wright: Yep. Any change goes through change control, shadow compare, and a rollout window.

[15:05] Priya Desai: Great.

[15:07] Ben Hartman: Can you explain “shadow compare” quickly.

[15:10] Logan Wright: It means we compute both old and new meter outputs from the same raw request logs. We compare drift. If it’s within expected bounds, we can flip it. If it’s not, we treat it like a bug. But invoices and customer-facing totals stay on the old until approved.

[15:30] Ben Hartman: Nice.

[15:31] Jordan Blake: Okay. I think we covered the conceptual stuff. Let’s talk logistics: Nadia sends the dictionary, Logan pulls the last 7 days cache hit and region breakdown, Priya sends 3 request_ids.

[15:45] Priya Desai: Yep.

[15:46] Sarah Kim: And I’ll reply with timezone preferences.

[15:49] Jordan Blake: Perfect. Before we wrap, any competitor comparisons you’re doing. Just so we can help.

[15:56] Priya Desai: We’re also testing OpenAI and Bedrock. Mainly just price predictability.

[16:03] Ben Hartman: And Anthropic for one flow.

[16:05] Jordan Blake: Got it. We’ll keep the focus on predictability and auditability.

[16:10] (crosstalk)

[16:11] Logan Wright: One more thing—when we enable the new fields, you might see columns like billed_tokens_total. If your parser breaks, ping us. We can also give you JSON API instead of CSV.

[16:23] Ben Hartman: JSON is better.

[16:25] Sarah Kim: CSV is easier for me, but we can do both.

[16:29] Jordan Blake: We’ll do both.

[16:31] Priya Desai: Can we schedule that working session with Logan/Nadia. Like Tuesday.

[16:36] Jordan Blake: Yep, I’ll send a hold. Tuesday 10am PT.

[16:41] Nadia Rahman: Works.

[16:42] Logan Wright: Works.

[16:44] Jordan Blake: Great. Quick recap:
1) Redwood enables usage API fields for billed tokens + cache attribution.
2) Send a sample export and a field dictionary.
3) Northwind sends request_ids for token diffs.
4) We do a working session to validate cache hit math and answer streaming edge questions.

[17:05] Sarah Kim: Sounds good.

[17:07] Priya Desai: Thanks.

[17:08] Ben Hartman: Thanks, all.

[17:10] Jordan Blake: Cool, talk soon.

(end)

Notes
- Transcript may contain minor inaccuracies due to automated transcription.
- “Cache hit” discussed refers to Redwood-side prefix/KV caching attribution as represented in usage events and exports.

[07:07] Nadia Rahman: Yep. Tokenization can vary by model family and tokenizer version. We use the tokenizer associated with the deployed model build. That’s why reconciling “our” tokens to “your” tokens only works if you use the same tokenizer implementation.

[07:24] Priya Desai: We do use a local tokenizer, but it’s probably not exactly the same.

[07:29] Logan Wright: That’s common. The best recon is request_id level: compare the usage export for a few known prompts and completions. If you can provide 2-3 request_ids where you see a difference, we can show you the raw_prompt_tokens/raw_completion_tokens from our side.

[07:47] Priya Desai: Okay, I can do that.

[07:50] Jordan Blake: Can we pivot to the dashboard for a second. Ben, you mentioned there’s a “cache hit rate” panel too?

[07:56] Ben Hartman: Yeah. It’s on the right, “Cache hit %” and “Cached prompt tokens”. It’s not super clear if that’s a rate of requests or tokens.

[08:06] Logan Wright: That’s a good callout. In v1, it’s token-based: cached prompt tokens divided by total prompt tokens. We’re adding a request-based hit rate as a separate metric, because it’s easier to reason about sometimes.

[08:22] Nadia Rahman: Also, the definition of “cache hit” can be partial. If the first 200 tokens hit but the rest is new, it still counts as a cache hit, but not 100% cached.

[08:42] Priya Desai: Exactly.

[08:44] Jordan Blake: So on the business side, you should see billed prompt tokens reduce proportional to cached prompt tokens. We can validate with a sample export.

[08:55] Sarah Kim: That’s what I want. Like, “show me a CSV with columns and I can do the math.”

[09:00] Jordan Blake: Great. We’ll do that.

[09:03] Priya Desai: One more thing. In the usage export we pulled last week, there was no cache field. Is that just not enabled.

[09:11] Logan Wright: Yeah. Those fields were behind a feature gate. We can enable it for your org. That will add columns; it won’t break existing parsing unless you’re strict about schema.

[09:24] Ben Hartman: We are strict.

[09:26] Jordan Blake: Okay, then we should version it. We can share a “v2 export schema” and you can switch when ready. In the API it’s additive fields, so older clients ignore them. But if you’re doing CSV ingestion with exact headers, we’ll coordinate.

[09:42] Sarah Kim: Please coordinate.

[09:45] Nadia Rahman: Also, there’s a distinction: usage API vs invoice. For POC you’re not getting invoices, but we’re aligning the usage API with what would be invoiced, meaning billed_tokens is what would map to charges.

[10:01] Priya Desai: Great.

[10:03] Ben Hartman: Can you confirm how region impacts pricing. Like if the dashboard says us-east sometimes, does that mean we got routed.

[10:11] Logan Wright: There is smart routing on capacity, but for POC we mostly keep you pinned to us-west unless we’re failing over. If you see another region, it might be either a fallback or a mis-tagged region dimension—there was a bug we fixed recently. But for your account, I can check.

[10:29] Jordan Blake: Logan, can you pull a seven-day report by region for them.

[10:33] Logan Wright: Yep.

[10:34] Priya Desai: We did see some “unknown” region values in export.

[10:38] Nadia Rahman: That’s exactly the missing dimension issue. It should be resolved, but there might be old events still.

[10:45] Sarah Kim: Old events as in last week.

[10:48] Logan Wright: Yeah, within the last couple weeks. We can backfill, but for your POC, we can just annotate it.

[10:56] Jordan Blake: Cool. Let’s talk about what you need to present internally. Priya, is it more performance or cost.

[11:03] Priya Desai: Both, but honestly the CFO question is “why is this line item unpredictable.” So the metering clarity is huge.

[11:12] Sarah Kim: Yes.

[11:13] Ben Hartman: We can handle the tokenization variance, but we need to understand what Redwood counts.

[11:19] Nadia Rahman: Then we should share a short dictionary:
- raw_prompt_tokens: tokenizer count for prompt
- raw_completion_tokens: tokenizer count for generated output
- billed_prompt_tokens: raw prompt tokens minus any cache-attributed tokens (or discounted, depending)
- billed_completion_tokens: typically equals raw completion tokens, minus de-duped retries
- cache_hit_prompt_tokens: portion of prompt tokens served from cache
- cache_hit: boolean if any portion of prompt was cache hit

[11:47] Priya Desai: Perfect. That’s what we need.

[11:50] Jordan Blake: Nadia, can you send that as an email after.

[11:53] Nadia Rahman: Yep.

[11:54] Ben Hartman: There’s still a question: do you ever bill for tokens generated during a server retry. Like if you generate 200 tokens, fail, retry, generate again.

[12:05] Logan Wright: We bill the successful request once, with de-dupe logic so internal attempts do not show up as multiple billed events. In the raw events, we may record attempts for debugging, but billing uses a stable idempotency key.

[12:20] Nadia Rahman: And for streaming, if the client disconnects, we’re working on making it consistent: do you bill only what was delivered, or what was generated. Current policy is bill what was delivered/confirmed, but it’s an area we’re tightening. That’s why you might see completion tokens 0 in some rare cases; we’d rather undercount than double count.

[12:40] Sarah Kim: Understood. That’s actually good.

[12:43] Priya Desai: We prefer that. The worst thing is arguing about phantom tokens.

[12:47] Jordan Blake: Exactly.

[12:49] Ben Hartman: Another small thing: the dashboard shows “Requests” but we have batched requests for embeddings in other systems. Not sure if that’s relevant here.

[12:58] Logan Wright: For chat/completions it’s 1 request equals 1 API call. For embeddings, if you send an array of inputs, we count it as 1 request but multiple “units” for embeddings. For your current POC you’re mostly chat, so not relevant.

[13:14] Priya Desai: Good.

[13:15] Jordan Blake: Let’s do a quick live readback. Ben, what’s the main concern you want closed by next week.

[13:21] Ben Hartman: I want a clear mapping between cache hit metrics and billed token reductions. If we can show one day’s worth of exports and compute it, we’re good.

[13:32] Priya Desai: And I want to confirm our internal “token per request” metrics aren’t wildly off because of tokenizer differences.

[13:40] Sarah Kim: And I want timezone alignment. The export day boundary is currently UTC, right.

[13:47] Logan Wright: Yes, it’s UTC by default.

[13:49] Sarah Kim: We operate on Pacific time. Otherwise daily charts look spiky.

[13:54] Jordan Blake: We’ve heard that before. We can either (a) provide timezone param in export, or (b) provide hourly exports and you roll up. Right now, easiest is hourly plus your rollup.

[14:09] Sarah Kim: Hourly is fine as long as it’s stable.

[14:12] Logan Wright: It’s stable, but there is still late arrival. We’ll note the freshness SLA.

[14:18] Nadia Rahman: And for month-end we can establish a “freeze” where after a certain time we stop backfilling without explicit approval. That’s more for paid contracts though.

[14:29] Sarah Kim: Good to know.

[14:31] Jordan Blake: We’ll keep it simple for the POC.

[14:34] Priya Desai: One more: are you planning to change these meter definitions soon. We don’t want the numbers shifting mid-test.

[14:43] Jordan Blake: Great question. The definitions are stabilizing. We’re adding fields, but we’re not changing how billed tokens are computed for you without notice. If there’s a meter fix, we’d run it in shadow mode first and tell you. Logan/Nadia can confirm.

[14:58] Logan Wright: Yep. Any change goes through change control, shadow compare, and a rollout window.

[15:05] Priya Desai: Great.

[15:07] Ben Hartman: Can you explain “shadow compare” quickly.

[15:10] Logan Wright: It means we compute both old and new meter outputs from the same raw request logs. We compare drift. If it’s within expected bounds, we can flip it. If it’s not, we treat it like a bug. But invoices and customer-facing totals stay on the old until approved.

[15:30] Ben Hartman: Nice.

[15:31] Jordan Blake: Okay. I think we covered the conceptual stuff. Let’s talk logistics: Nadia sends the dictionary, Logan pulls the last 7 days cache hit and region breakdown, Priya sends 3 request_ids.

[15:45] Priya Desai: Yep.

[15:46] Sarah Kim: And I’ll reply with timezone preferences.

[15:49] Jordan Blake: Perfect. Before we wrap, any competitor comparisons you’re doing. Just so we can help.

[15:56] Priya Desai: We’re also testing OpenAI and Bedrock. Mainly just price predictability.

[16:03] Ben Hartman: And Anthropic for one flow.

[16:05] Jordan Blake: Got it. We’ll keep the focus on predictability and auditability.

[16:10] (crosstalk)

[16:11] Logan Wright: One more thing—when we enable the new fields, you might see columns like billed_tokens_total. If your parser breaks, ping us. We can also give you JSON API instead of CSV.

[16:23] Ben Hartman: JSON is better.

[16:25] Sarah Kim: CSV is easier for me, but we can do both.

[16:29] Jordan Blake: We’ll do both.

[16:31] Priya Desai: Can we schedule that working session with Logan/Nadia. Like Tuesday.

[16:36] Jordan Blake: Yep, I’ll send a hold. Tuesday 10am PT.

[16:41] Nadia Rahman: Works.

[16:42] Logan Wright: Works.

[16:44] Jordan Blake: Great. Quick recap:
1) Redwood enables usage API fields for billed tokens + cache attribution.
2) Send a sample export and a field dictionary.
3) Northwind sends request_ids for token diffs.
4) We do a working session to validate cache hit math and answer streaming edge questions.

[17:05] Sarah Kim: Sounds good.

[17:07] Priya Desai: Thanks.

[17:08] Ben Hartman: Thanks, all.

[17:10] Jordan Blake: Cool, talk soon.

title: Tensorlytics x Redwood — latency + observability technical deep dive
preview:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Meeting: Tensorlytics x Redwood — latency + observability technical deep dive
Date: 2025-11-14
Start time: 4:00 PM PT
Duration: 53 min
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Marissa Cole, Noah Patel, Logan Wright, Allison Grant
Tensorlytics: Jordan Lee, Priya Nair, Alex Chen, Mina Sato

Auto-generated Summary (Fireflies)
- Tensorlytics asked how Redwood detects latency regressions (p95/p99) and throughput drops (tokens/sec) per model, and what the operational response looks like.
- Redwood described their always-on production performance canary: synthetic prompt shapes, low overhead budget, per-model baselines, and tiered alerting with anti-flap and dedupe.
- Discussion covered metric breakdowns (queueing vs runtime execution, prefill vs decode), attribution signals (kernel tags, runtime build id, GPU SKU), and how canary signals can gate rollouts.
- Next steps: share SLO/alert examples, align on benchmark shapes and regions, and plan a limited pilot with a small model set.

Topics
1) Tensorlytics latency requirements and current pain points
2) Redwood latency SLOs: definitions, p95 vs p99, end-to-end vs runtime-only
3) Perf canary design: synthetic workloads, baselines, regression thresholds
4) Alerting + on-call response: warning vs paging, runbooks, rollback gates
5) Metrics cardinality + data handling (no customer prompts)

Action items (captured)
- Redwood (Logan): Send example Grafana dashboard screenshots / panel list for per-model p95, tokens/sec, queueing time, batch-size distribution. (Due: 2025-11-18)
- Redwood (Allison): Send alert severity policy and what triggers paging vs a warning. (Due: 2025-11-18)
- Redwood (Noah): Share a sanitized sample of an incident-bot alert payload with baseline vs observed and links. (Due: 2025-11-18)
- Tensorlytics (Alex): Provide current latency SLO targets and how they compute p95/p99 (windowing) + existing pager thresholds. (Due: 2025-11-20)
- Tensorlytics (Mina): Provide representative prompt length buckets and typical output lengths for 2 workloads (chat + extraction). (Due: 2025-11-20)

Transcript

[00:00] Marissa Cole: Cool, we’ll get started. Thanks everyone for making time. This is Marissa, I’m PM on rollouts and canaries.

[00:07] Jordan Lee: Hey, Jordan here. Thanks. We’ve been, uh, burned recently by a latency regression and we’re trying to understand how you all handle that in prod.

[00:15] Marissa Cole: Yep, makes sense. Maybe quick intros on our side—Noah runs the perf canary work, Logan owns telemetry, Allison is SRE standards.

[00:24] Noah Patel: Hey all.

[00:25] Logan Wright: Hi.

[00:26] Allison Grant: Hey.

[00:28] Priya Nair: Priya, platform. Alex is SRE. Mina is ML.

[00:33] Alex Chen: Hi.

[00:34] Mina Sato: Hello.

[00:37] Marissa Cole: Great. Jordan, want to level-set what “bad day” looks like for you? Like p95 targets, regions, and what happened last time.

[00:47] Jordan Lee: Sure. We run a real-time assistant in-app. Customers notice if p95 goes above, like, 900 milliseconds end-to-end. p99 is also important but we alert on p95 mostly. Last month we had a model update—supposedly “minor”—and our tokens per second dropped, so responses got… sticky. It wasn’t a full outage, but we basically had to scale up to hide it.

[01:13] Priya Nair: And we don’t want to be the first to find out. Like, we want you to catch it before it rolls to our traffic.

[01:20] Marissa Cole: Yep. That’s exactly the class of problem the canary is meant to catch.

[01:24] Alex Chen: When you say canary, is this like shadow traffic? Because we can’t have customer prompts leaving our env, and also, even if it’s in your hosted, we don’t want prompts logged.

[01:37] Noah Patel: Good question. The canary we’re talking about is not customer shadowing. It’s synthetic prompts, like prompt “shapes” that match production distributions. We don’t need your text. We’re measuring performance of the serving runtime.

[01:52] Alex Chen: Shapes meaning… length and output length?

[01:56] Noah Patel: Exactly. Prompt length buckets, max new tokens buckets, and the mix of prefill vs decode. We generate safe filler text, and it’s seedable so runs are reproducible.

[02:10] Mina Sato: So you’re benchmarking with like lorem ipsum.

[02:13] Noah Patel: Yeah, basically. It’s not about semantics. It’s about the runtime path. We care about tail latency and throughput, and regressions from kernel selection, scheduling heuristics, model config, or GPU fleet changes.

[02:30] Logan Wright: And to Alex’s logging point, we have explicit redaction and “no PII” assertions in the canary. It’s also a restricted service account. The payload is synthetic, and we don’t store prompt content. Metrics are aggregate.

[02:45] Priya Nair: Okay. How does it actually detect regressions? Like if there’s natural variance, we don’t want pages all the time.

[02:54] Allison Grant: Totally. We structure it as tiered alerting: warning vs paging, with anti-flap. We require a minimum sample size and sustained deviation beyond threshold.

[03:06] Alex Chen: Sustained like… five minutes, 30 minutes?

[03:10] Allison Grant: Depends on severity. For warning, it might be “two out of the last three windows” plus min samples. For paging, it’s usually longer and only for top models. We also dedupe in incident-bot so you don’t get storms.

[03:27] Jordan Lee: Can you define “top models” in your world? Is it per customer or global.

[03:33] Marissa Cole: It’s global for the platform, like the most-used and highest risk ones. But for Dedicated, we can also make sure your pinned model versions are included in the canary coverage in your regions.

[03:48] Priya Nair: We care about us-east and eu-west. Also, we’re exploring Dedicated because we have spiky daytime traffic.

[03:55] Marissa Cole: Okay. That’s a good fit. Let’s get into specifics on metrics and baselines.

[04:02] Noah Patel: The canary emits p50/p95/p99 end-to-end latency, and runtime-only latency. It also breaks down prefill latency, decode latency, queueing time, and batch size distribution. Tokens per second is tracked both overall and per decode portion.

[04:23] Alex Chen: Runtime-only latency meaning from after it hits your gateway?

[04:27] Noah Patel: Yeah. Roughly: request accepted by inference service to completion, excluding some outer network overhead. Then end-to-end includes gateway and any auth/route hops.

[04:38] Logan Wright: We also attach dimensions—carefully—like model, model version, runtime build id, kernel tag, region, and hardware SKU. We keep label cardinality under control because it can explode.

[04:53] Priya Nair: Kernel tag as in the CUDA kernel selection?

[04:56] Noah Patel: Yeah. For attention kernels, GEMM, different fused paths. If we change the kernel selection logic, we want a direct correlation in telemetry. So the canary can say “regression started when kernel_tag changed from X to Y,” which helps triage.

[05:14] Jordan Lee: That’s nice. Our last incident was super hard to attribute. We were guessing between networking, model, or GPU node type.

[05:23] Allison Grant: This is where queueing time is also huge. If p95 goes up but runtime stays flat and queueing spikes, that’s often capacity pressure, scheduling, or a noisy neighbor issue, not a kernel regression.

[05:38] Alex Chen: Are you able to separate queueing time from compute consistently?

[05:42] Noah Patel: We instrument the scheduler. It’s “time in queue before batch execution.” It’s not perfect in every edge case, but it’s a strong signal. We validate it under load.

[05:55] Mina Sato: And tokens per second, how do you define it? We’ve seen vendors define it differently.

[06:02] Noah Patel: Yeah, fair. We define it as generated tokens divided by decode wall time (and also track end-to-end tokens/sec). We make the definition explicit in the dashboard.

[06:13] Jordan Lee: Okay, so what triggers the baseline? Like do you compare to yesterday? Or a fixed number.

[06:21] Logan Wright: We use versioned baselines. Conceptually: per model + runtime version + hardware SKU, and optionally region. We store baseline distributions derived from controlled load runs. Then the canary compares observed stats against that baseline.

[06:40] Alex Chen: So if your fleet slowly drifts, doesn’t the baseline become stale and alert all the time.

[06:47] Logan Wright: That’s the baseline drift problem, yes. We’re careful about when baselines update. We don’t do a naive rolling baseline that chases the regression.

[06:57] Allison Grant: Operationally, we treat baseline updates like a change. It’s versioned and auditable. And we cap how fast it can move. Plus, the alert logic has dampening.

[07:09] Priya Nair: Could you walk us through an example incident? Like, canary fires, what happens next.

[07:15] Marissa Cole: Sure. Typically incident-bot posts into a channel thread. Warning goes to #eng-runtime. Paging goes to on-call plus runtime.

[07:25] Allison Grant: The alert includes: model, region, observed p95 vs baseline p95, percent change, confidence / sample size, and direct links: dashboard panel, recent deploys, and a runbook.

[07:40] Alex Chen: Does it also include what changed? Like “runtime build id bumped.”

[07:44] Logan Wright: Yes, we attach the runtime build id and kernel tag in the payload. We also link to recent rollout events.

[07:52] Noah Patel: Then triage is usually: 1) confirm it’s not canary noise—check sample size, check if it’s isolated to a region or SKU. 2) check breakdown: queueing vs prefill vs decode. 3) check if kernel tag changed. 4) correlate with fleet changes or release.

[08:10] Jordan Lee: And mitigation?

[08:12] Noah Patel: If it’s a runtime release, we can rollback runtime in that region. If it’s a kernel feature flag, we can flip it off. If it’s capacity, we can adjust scheduling/batching knobs or shift traffic to other pools.

[08:29] Priya Nair: When you say shift traffic, is that on your side or customer side.

[08:33] Marissa Cole: In hosted, it’s on our side. In Dedicated, depends on contract and how isolated the pool is, but we can still do regional fallback if you configure it.

[08:44] Alex Chen: This gets into rollout gates. Do you actually block deployments based on the canary.

[08:50] Marissa Cole: For some classes of runtime and kernel rollouts, yes—we’re moving toward “gates” where sustained canary regression will automatically pause expansion or require explicit override.

[09:04] Jordan Lee: We like that. We’ve had vendors say “we watch dashboards” but that doesn’t stop anything.

[09:09] Allison Grant: To set expectations, we don’t want the gate to flap. So the gate criteria are stricter than warning alerts. It’s like a higher confidence threshold.

[09:20] Priya Nair: What are the actual thresholds, like percent regression.

[09:24] Noah Patel: It depends per model. But as a rough order, you might do something like: warning at +8–10% p95 sustained, page at +15–20% for top models, and similarly for tokens/sec drops, like -10% warning, -20% page. Those are placeholders. We tune based on observed variance.

[09:47] Mina Sato: Do different prompt lengths have different baselines? Because our workload is bimodal.

[09:53] Noah Patel: Great point. We bucket prompt lengths and output lengths. The canary workload is a mixture. We also track those distributions so you can see if the batch is heavy on long prompts that day.

[10:08] Logan Wright: But we’re careful not to explode labels by having per-bucket labels everywhere. Mostly we do histograms and a small number of coarse buckets.

[10:19] Alex Chen: Can you share what “small number” means.

[10:22] Logan Wright: Typically 4–6 prompt length buckets and 3–4 output buckets, but in metrics we store them as histograms rather than as a high-cardinality label set.

[10:35] Priya Nair: Okay, I want to ask about overhead. If this canary is always running, is it stealing GPU capacity from customers.

[10:43] Noah Patel: We have a strict overhead budget. Target is under 0.5% GPU capacity per region for covered models. We enforce QPS caps and a “capacity budget” in the canary service.

[10:58] Jordan Lee: So if you add models, it doesn’t like explode.

[11:01] Noah Patel: Right, there are safeguards to prevent accidental wide fan-out. Config is validated.

[11:07] Alex Chen: Do you run it per region and per GPU type.

[11:11] Noah Patel: Yes, because regressions can be SKU-specific.

[11:15] Priya Nair: That’s important. We’ve seen A100 vs H100 behave differently with the same model.

[11:20] Noah Patel: Exactly.

[11:22] Marissa Cole: Maybe we can pause and ask—what’s your internal SLO policy? Like, do you page on p95, p99.

[11:30] Alex Chen: We page on p95 end-to-end for a few endpoints. p99 is more like a dashboard metric, but it’s the one customers complain about.

[11:39] Jordan Lee: We also care about tokens/sec because it affects user feel in streaming. If it starts slow, people think it’s broken.

[11:47] Noah Patel: That maps well. We track streaming first-token latency too, which often correlates with prefill plus queueing.

[11:56] Mina Sato: First token latency, yes.

[11:59] Logan Wright: Just to clarify, we might call it “time to first token” in the dashboard. Same idea.

[12:05] Priya Nair: Is your SLO expressed as “p95 under X” per model.

[12:10] Allison Grant: Internally we treat it like an SLO-like signal per model, yes. But we also separate “customer-facing SLO” from “regression detection.” The canary is mainly for regressions against baseline, not absolute thresholds.

[12:27] Alex Chen: That’s subtle. So you could have a model that is kind of slow but stable, and it wouldn’t alert.

[12:32] Allison Grant: Correct.

[12:35] Jordan Lee: But for us, we need both. Like absolute SLO and regression.

[12:40] Marissa Cole: Yep. For Dedicated we can align on absolute performance targets and then the canary is like guardrails against changes.

[12:49] Priya Nair: Are canary results visible to customers? Or only internal.

[12:54] Marissa Cole: Today, primarily internal. But we can share relevant dashboard views during an incident, and we’re working on surfacing more in the console over time.

[13:05] Alex Chen: I’d like at least a way to see “is the platform experiencing a regression” without opening a ticket.

[13:10] Marissa Cole: Understood. We can talk about what we can expose during a pilot.

[13:17] Mina Sato: On prompt shapes: do you model tool calling? That can change token patterns.

[13:22] Noah Patel: For the initial canary we keep it mostly plain generation because tool calling is more about the application-level tokens. But we can include structured output parameters, like forcing JSON, because that can change decode behavior a bit.

[13:38] Jordan Lee: Another question: how do you prevent the canary from masking issues by caching? Like if KV cache is hitting.

[13:46] Noah Patel: Good question. We can run both cache-on and cache-off variants, depending on what signal we want. Also we track KV cache hit rate where applicable.

[13:58] Logan Wright: And that hit rate is a metric, not a label. Again cardinality.

[14:03] Alex Chen: You mentioned you had issues with cardinality. What happened.

[14:08] Logan Wright: Early on, people wanted too many dimensions—like model + model version + prompt bucket + customer tier + route + region + SKU… it gets expensive and noisy. We did a cardinality audit and narrowed down the label set.

[14:27] Priya Nair: We’ve had Prometheus bills explode from that.

[14:30] Logan Wright: Yeah exactly.

[14:33] Marissa Cole: We’re at about 15 minutes. Let’s dig into the operational response more since that’s your key concern.

[14:40] Jordan Lee: Yeah. So, pretend it’s Friday night, p95 regresses 20% for the model we’re on. What happens, realistically.

[14:50] Allison Grant: If it’s a top model and it’s sustained with enough samples, it pages on-call. The first check is “is this isolated to one region/SKU.” If yes, mitigation might be to shift traffic away or to drain that pool.

[15:07] Noah Patel: If it correlates with a runtime release, we rollback. We keep rollbacks fast—like minutes—because runtime changes are usually controlled by rollout tooling.

[15:18] Alex Chen: Do you do automatic rollback.

[15:21] Marissa Cole: Not fully automatic across the board yet. We do auto-pause for some. Full auto-rollback is something we’re cautious about because we don’t want to trade one incident for another. But we do have a “halt expansion” mechanism.

[15:38] Jordan Lee: Makes sense.

[15:40] Priya Nair: How do you decide it’s kernel vs scheduler.

[15:45] Noah Patel: The breakdown. If queueing increases, likely scheduler/capacity. If runtime execution per token increases and kernel tag changed, likely kernel. If only prefill changes, it might be model config like rope scaling or context length settings. If only decode slows, could be KV cache behavior or kernel.

[16:08] Mina Sato: Rope scaling? Sorry.

[16:10] Noah Patel: Sorry, RoPE is positional embeddings scaling. Some config changes can affect attention compute.

[16:17] Mina Sato: Got it.

[16:19] Logan Wright: Also the payload includes runtime build id. So you can correlate to a specific release.

[16:26] Alex Chen: Can you show us the payload.

[16:28] Noah Patel: I can’t show in this call because I don’t have it pulled up, but we’ll send a sanitized example. It’s basically: “model=…, region=…, baseline_p95=…, observed_p95=…, delta=…, samples=…,” plus links.

[16:45] Jordan Lee: Links to what.

[16:47] Allison Grant: Grafana panels, trace explorer, and the runbook.

[16:51] Priya Nair: Do you have tracing in the inference path.

[16:54] Logan Wright: We have tracing hooks. For canary traffic, it’s straightforward because it’s tagged as canary. For customer traffic, depends on deployment mode, but we can typically provide correlation ids.

[17:09] Alex Chen: How do you make sure canary traffic is isolated. Like it doesn’t get batched with customer traffic.

[17:15] Noah Patel: It can be configured. In many cases, it shares the same scheduler because we want it to detect the real behavior, including batching heuristics. But we tag it so we can separate metrics. It still consumes minimal QPS.

[17:30] Priya Nair: I can see pros and cons. If it shares scheduler, it sees the real thing. But it could also be impacted by load.

[17:37] Noah Patel: Exactly. That’s why we look at runtime-only vs queueing. If it’s impacted by load, queueing will show it.

[17:48] Jordan Lee: Do you run the canary during peak times.

[17:51] Noah Patel: It’s always on, but rate limited. It gives us coverage across time-of-day effects.

[18:00] Alex Chen: What’s the sampling window.

[18:02] Noah Patel: Depends on model. We tune to get enough samples without exceeding budget. Usually we want at least hundreds of requests per evaluation window for a given model-region-SKU combination.

[18:16] Logan Wright: And if we don’t have enough samples, we don’t alert. That prevents spurious alarms.

[18:22] Mina Sato: For our workloads, we often have long contexts. Do you test long context.

[18:28] Noah Patel: We have long-context buckets, but we may not cover extreme lengths for every model due to cost. For a Dedicated pilot, we can add a “high risk” profile for your pinned model.

[18:44] Priya Nair: That would matter. Our extraction workload can be 8k input tokens.

[18:48] Noah Patel: Yep, then prefill dominates. We should absolutely include that shape.

[18:55] Jordan Lee: Bringing it back to SLA. Do you have a formal latency SLO.

[19:01] Allison Grant: We don’t publish a single global number because it’s model and deployment dependent. But we do commit to performance targets in Dedicated contracts and we operate with internal SLOs per model and tier.

[19:18] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[19:20] Priya Nair: If we pin to a model version, how do you handle a security patch that changes runtime.

[19:26] Marissa Cole: That’s where gates and change management come in. We can patch runtime while keeping the model pinned. Canary catches regressions from the runtime change. If it regresses, we pause/rollback.

[19:40] Alex Chen: And you notify customers.

[19:42] Marissa Cole: For major incidents yes, and for Dedicated we can do proactive comms.

[19:47] Jordan Lee: We had an incident with another provider where they rolled a driver update and p99 went crazy, and support said “we’re investigating.” It lasted like 6 hours.

[19:58] Noah Patel: Driver and fleet changes are exactly the kind of thing canary catches if it impacts performance. The hardware SKU dimension helps, and we also correlate to fleet events.

[20:10] Logan Wright: We track “fleet change” as an event stream, so you can overlay it on charts.

[20:16] Priya Nair: That’s helpful.

[20:18] Mina Sato: Can you talk about batching heuristics. We’ve seen batch size changes cause latency spikes.

[20:24] Noah Patel: Yes. Continuous batching is sensitive. The canary tracks batch size distribution and queueing. If a heuristic change makes batches deeper, tokens/sec might improve but p95 might worsen, or vice versa.

[20:41] Alex Chen: And you can alert on either.

[20:43] Noah Patel: Yes. We watch p95 and tokens/sec at minimum.

[20:49] Allison Grant: And we have separate alert policies because you don’t want to page on every 3% change.

[20:55] Jordan Lee: Okay. What about p99 though.

[20:58] Allison Grant: We track it, but alerting on p99 can be noisy. We usually use it as a corroborating signal and for deeper triage.

[21:10] Priya Nair: Same experience here.

[21:12] Marissa Cole: Maybe we should cover Dedicated specifics since you mentioned it.

[21:18] Priya Nair: Sure.

[21:20] Marissa Cole: In Dedicated, you have reserved GPU pools. That improves predictability. Canary can run against that pool, which gives you a “pool health” signal. It can also distinguish “hosted fleet issue” vs “your pool issue.”

[21:37] Alex Chen: That’s good. In the shared environment, it’s hard to know if the issue is us or everyone.

[21:42] Logan Wright: We’d still keep the metric dimensions limited to avoid per-customer cardinality issues. But for Dedicated, it’s more manageable since it’s a small number of pools.

[21:54] Jordan Lee: How about private deployment. Not immediate but future.

[21:58] Marissa Cole: Private can run the same canary service inside your VPC, if desired, with the same synthetic prompts. Then baselines are specific to your hardware.

[22:10] Alex Chen: Would that require your control plane to receive metrics.

[22:14] Logan Wright: It can. Or metrics can stay in your observability stack. But for managed operations we’d need some aggregated signal.

[22:25] Priya Nair: Okay.

[22:27] Mina Sato: Quick question: do you measure “time to first token” separate from “time to last token.”

[22:33] Noah Patel: We do. It often maps to prefill + queue. Then the rest is decode throughput.

[22:41] Jordan Lee: That’s basically user experience.

[22:44] Noah Patel: Yep.

[22:46] Alex Chen: What happens if the telemetry pipeline is delayed. Like metrics ingestion lags and alerts come late.

[22:52] Logan Wright: It’s a real issue. We monitor pipeline latency and we annotate dashboards. Also, alerting can have a “data freshness” guard, so it won’t fire based on partial data.

[23:07] Allison Grant: Otherwise you get weird late alerts and flapping. We’ve learned that the hard way.

[23:12] Priya Nair: Good.

[23:14] Marissa Cole: Jordan, do you want to talk about timeline. Are you looking to evaluate soon.

[23:20] Jordan Lee: Yes. We’re trying to decide in December. We want a pilot in us-east first. If that goes well, eu-west.

[23:28] Marissa Cole: Great. For pilot we’d propose a limited model set and we can show you the canary signals and how we respond.

[23:38] Alex Chen: One concern: we don’t want your canary to say “green” but our workload to be “red.”

[23:44] Noah Patel: Totally. The canary is a guardrail, not a replacement for workload-specific benchmarks. That’s why we want your prompt shape distribution—lengths, output lengths, concurrency. We can calibrate the canary profile to be closer to your workloads.

[24:03] Mina Sato: We can provide that without content.

[24:06] Noah Patel: Exactly.

[24:08] Priya Nair: When you say concurrency, do you mean QPS.

[24:11] Noah Patel: QPS and number of concurrent requests. Both matter with batching.

[24:18] Logan Wright: And we can run a controlled benchmark run to generate baselines for your pilot model.

[24:26] Jordan Lee: That would be helpful. The last vendor basically said “just try it.”

[24:32] Marissa Cole: We try not to do that.

[24:34] Alex Chen: Can you confirm the alert routing. Like who gets paged.

[24:39] Allison Grant: Internally, our on-call rotation gets paged for paging-level alerts. Runtime team is looped in. For warnings, it goes to the runtime engineering channel. If it’s a Dedicated pool issue, we also include the SRE sponsor.

[24:58] Alex Chen: Okay.

[25:00] Priya Nair: And do you have a runbook. Like an actual decision tree.

[25:04] Allison Grant: Yes. It starts with validating the alert, checking whether it’s end-to-end vs runtime-only, checking queueing, checking release/fleet events, then recommends mitigations and escalation paths.

[25:18] Jordan Lee: Can we see it.

[25:20] Allison Grant: We’ll send a sanitized version.

[25:24] Mina Sato: Any chance the canary could leak information about our usage. Like model list.

[25:29] Logan Wright: Canary model list is internal per region and tier. For your Dedicated pool, the models are yours. But the metrics are not public. Access is controlled.

[25:41] Priya Nair: Okay.

[25:43] Marissa Cole: Let’s do a quick lightning round on competitors and what you’ve evaluated. Mostly to understand expectations.

[25:51] Jordan Lee: We use OpenAI today for some things, but cost is high. We’ve tried Anthropic. We also experimented with vLLM self-hosted but ops burden is high.

[26:03] Priya Nair: On AWS, we looked at SageMaker endpoints, but again, we end up building everything ourselves.

[26:11] Marissa Cole: Makes sense.

[26:13] Alex Chen: For the record, the self-hosted vLLM issue was we didn’t have good regression detection. We basically noticed when customers complained.

[26:22] Noah Patel: Yep. That’s the gap.

[26:25] Jordan Lee: Okay, so what’s your biggest known limitation with the canary today.

[26:31] Noah Patel: The honest answer: early on, baseline drift and alert noise were issues. We’ve tightened baseline versioning and anti-flap logic. Also, canary coverage starts small and we expand; so if a model isn’t covered, it won’t catch it.

[26:52] Logan Wright: And we have to balance metric richness with telemetry cost.

[26:56] Allison Grant: From on-call perspective, we’re careful about paging hygiene.

[27:01] Priya Nair: Okay.

[27:03] Alex Chen: Do you have a “disable switch” if canary itself causes problems.

[27:07] Noah Patel: Yes, there’s a safe disable. It stops canary traffic without impacting inference endpoints. Also QPS caps prevent it from doing damage.

[27:20] Jordan Lee: Good.

[27:22] Marissa Cole: We’re around the halfway point. Anything else you want to go deep on—dashboards, definitions, or rollout gates.

[27:30] Priya Nair: Dashboards. Specifically, do you show “batch size distribution” and “queue time” on the same view.

[27:36] Logan Wright: Yes. The per-model dashboard has panels for p95, tokens/sec, and then breakdown panels for prefill, decode, queueing, batch size distribution. We also show KV cache hit rate when relevant.

[27:55] Alex Chen: And can I filter by region.

[27:58] Logan Wright: Yes.

[28:00] Mina Sato: Do you chart it per model version.

[28:03] Logan Wright: We can, but we keep model version as a dimension you can select rather than a label on every time series. Again cost.

[28:14] Priya Nair: Okay.

[28:16] Jordan Lee: What about alerts. Do they include the dashboard link with the correct filter pre-applied.

[28:22] Allison Grant: Yes, that’s part of the standard. The link includes model and region query params so it opens to the right view.

[28:32] Alex Chen: Nice. I hate generic links.

[28:35] Noah Patel: Same.

[28:37] Marissa Cole: Okay. For next steps, we’ll send: example dashboards, alert policy, and an alert payload. And you’ll send prompt shape distribution and your SLO targets. Then we’ll schedule a benchmark planning session.

[28:52] Jordan Lee: Sounds good.

[28:54] Priya Nair: One more question. If you catch a regression, do you ever proactively pause all releases.

[29:01] Allison Grant: If it’s severe and unclear, yes. We can do a temporary freeze, but we try to scope it. We don’t want to stop unrelated changes forever.

[29:12] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[29:14] Alex Chen: If we’re on Dedicated, do we get notified if you freeze.

[29:18] Marissa Cole: Yes, for changes that affect your pool or your pinned versions.

[29:24] Mina Sato: Do you do A/B canary. Like canary gets new kernel first.

[29:28] Noah Patel: Yes, for risky changes we do limited rollouts. Canary is also part of that because it exercises the new runtime before broad customer impact.

[29:40] Priya Nair: So it’s like canary-on-canary.

[29:42] Noah Patel: Kind of, yeah.

[29:44] Logan Wright: And we annotate the charts with rollout events.

[29:49] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[29:51] Marissa Cole: I think we have what we need. Any final concerns.

[29:56] Alex Chen: Just make sure the canary doesn’t use any of our data. But it sounds like it doesn’t.

[30:02] Logan Wright: Correct.

[30:04] Priya Nair: And we’d like region-specific baselines. We’ve seen eu-west behave differently.

[30:10] Noah Patel: Agreed. Region and SKU are important dimensions.

[30:14] Jordan Lee: Great.

[30:16] Marissa Cole: Cool, thanks everyone. We’ll follow up with docs and schedule the benchmark planning session.

[30:22] Jordan Lee: Thanks.

[30:23] Priya Nair: Thanks.

[30:24] Alex Chen: Thank you.

[30:25] Mina Sato: Thanks.

(Transcript ends early due to recording stop; remaining ~22 minutes was Q&A on Dedicated sizing and pricing, not captured clearly.)
text:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Meeting: Tensorlytics x Redwood — latency + observability technical deep dive
Date: 2025-11-14
Start time: 4:00 PM PT
Duration: 53 min
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Marissa Cole, Noah Patel, Logan Wright, Allison Grant
Tensorlytics: Jordan Lee, Priya Nair, Alex Chen, Mina Sato

Auto-generated Summary (Fireflies)
- Tensorlytics asked how Redwood detects latency regressions (p95/p99) and throughput drops (tokens/sec) per model, and what the operational response looks like.
- Redwood described their always-on production performance canary: synthetic prompt shapes, low overhead budget, per-model baselines, and tiered alerting with anti-flap and dedupe.
- Discussion covered metric breakdowns (queueing vs runtime execution, prefill vs decode), attribution signals (kernel tags, runtime build id, GPU SKU), and how canary signals can gate rollouts.
- Next steps: share SLO/alert examples, align on benchmark shapes and regions, and plan a limited pilot with a small model set.

Topics
1) Tensorlytics latency requirements and current pain points
2) Redwood latency SLOs: definitions, p95 vs p99, end-to-end vs runtime-only
3) Perf canary design: synthetic workloads, baselines, regression thresholds
4) Alerting + on-call response: warning vs paging, runbooks, rollback gates
5) Metrics cardinality + data handling (no customer prompts)

Action items (captured)
- Redwood (Logan): Send example Grafana dashboard screenshots / panel list for per-model p95, tokens/sec, queueing time, batch-size distribution. (Due: 2025-11-18)
- Redwood (Allison): Send alert severity policy and what triggers paging vs a warning. (Due: 2025-11-18)
- Redwood (Noah): Share a sanitized sample of an incident-bot alert payload with baseline vs observed and links. (Due: 2025-11-18)
- Tensorlytics (Alex): Provide current latency SLO targets and how they compute p95/p99 (windowing) + existing pager thresholds. (Due: 2025-11-20)
- Tensorlytics (Mina): Provide representative prompt length buckets and typical output lengths for 2 workloads (chat + extraction). (Due: 2025-11-20)

Transcript

[00:00] Marissa Cole: Cool, we’ll get started. Thanks everyone for making time. This is Marissa, I’m PM on rollouts and canaries.

[00:07] Jordan Lee: Hey, Jordan here. Thanks. We’ve been, uh, burned recently by a latency regression and we’re trying to understand how you all handle that in prod.

[00:15] Marissa Cole: Yep, makes sense. Maybe quick intros on our side—Noah runs the perf canary work, Logan owns telemetry, Allison is SRE standards.

[00:24] Noah Patel: Hey all.

[00:25] Logan Wright: Hi.

[00:26] Allison Grant: Hey.

[00:28] Priya Nair: Priya, platform. Alex is SRE. Mina is ML.

[00:33] Alex Chen: Hi.

[00:34] Mina Sato: Hello.

[00:37] Marissa Cole: Great. Jordan, want to level-set what “bad day” looks like for you? Like p95 targets, regions, and what happened last time.

[00:47] Jordan Lee: Sure. We run a real-time assistant in-app. Customers notice if p95 goes above, like, 900 milliseconds end-to-end. p99 is also important but we alert on p95 mostly. Last month we had a model update—supposedly “minor”—and our tokens per second dropped, so responses got… sticky. It wasn’t a full outage, but we basically had to scale up to hide it.

[01:13] Priya Nair: And we don’t want to be the first to find out. Like, we want you to catch it before it rolls to our traffic.

[01:20] Marissa Cole: Yep. That’s exactly the class of problem the canary is meant to catch.

[01:24] Alex Chen: When you say canary, is this like shadow traffic? Because we can’t have customer prompts leaving our env, and also, even if it’s in your hosted, we don’t want prompts logged.

[01:37] Noah Patel: Good question. The canary we’re talking about is not customer shadowing. It’s synthetic prompts, like prompt “shapes” that match production distributions. We don’t need your text. We’re measuring performance of the serving runtime.

[01:52] Alex Chen: Shapes meaning… length and output length?

[01:56] Noah Patel: Exactly. Prompt length buckets, max new tokens buckets, and the mix of prefill vs decode. We generate safe filler text, and it’s seedable so runs are reproducible.

[02:10] Mina Sato: So you’re benchmarking with like lorem ipsum.

[02:13] Noah Patel: Yeah, basically. It’s not about semantics. It’s about the runtime path. We care about tail latency and throughput, and regressions from kernel selection, scheduling heuristics, model config, or GPU fleet changes.

[02:30] Logan Wright: And to Alex’s logging point, we have explicit redaction and “no PII” assertions in the canary. It’s also a restricted service account. The payload is synthetic, and we don’t store prompt content. Metrics are aggregate.

[02:45] Priya Nair: Okay. How does it actually detect regressions? Like if there’s natural variance, we don’t want pages all the time.

[02:54] Allison Grant: Totally. We structure it as tiered alerting: warning vs paging, with anti-flap. We require a minimum sample size and sustained deviation beyond threshold.

[03:06] Alex Chen: Sustained like… five minutes, 30 minutes?

[03:10] Allison Grant: Depends on severity. For warning, it might be “two out of the last three windows” plus min samples. For paging, it’s usually longer and only for top models. We also dedupe in incident-bot so you don’t get storms.

[03:27] Jordan Lee: Can you define “top models” in your world? Is it per customer or global.

[03:33] Marissa Cole: It’s global for the platform, like the most-used and highest risk ones. But for Dedicated, we can also make sure your pinned model versions are included in the canary coverage in your regions.

[03:48] Priya Nair: We care about us-east and eu-west. Also, we’re exploring Dedicated because we have spiky daytime traffic.

[03:55] Marissa Cole: Okay. That’s a good fit. Let’s get into specifics on metrics and baselines.

[04:02] Noah Patel: The canary emits p50/p95/p99 end-to-end latency, and runtime-only latency. It also breaks down prefill latency, decode latency, queueing time, and batch size distribution. Tokens per second is tracked both overall and per decode portion.

[04:23] Alex Chen: Runtime-only latency meaning from after it hits your gateway?

[04:27] Noah Patel: Yeah. Roughly: request accepted by inference service to completion, excluding some outer network overhead. Then end-to-end includes gateway and any auth/route hops.

[04:38] Logan Wright: We also attach dimensions—carefully—like model, model version, runtime build id, kernel tag, region, and hardware SKU. We keep label cardinality under control because it can explode.

[04:53] Priya Nair: Kernel tag as in the CUDA kernel selection?

[04:56] Noah Patel: Yeah. For attention kernels, GEMM, different fused paths. If we change the kernel selection logic, we want a direct correlation in telemetry. So the canary can say “regression started when kernel_tag changed from X to Y,” which helps triage.

[05:14] Jordan Lee: That’s nice. Our last incident was super hard to attribute. We were guessing between networking, model, or GPU node type.

[05:23] Allison Grant: This is where queueing time is also huge. If p95 goes up but runtime stays flat and queueing spikes, that’s often capacity pressure, scheduling, or a noisy neighbor issue, not a kernel regression.

[05:38] Alex Chen: Are you able to separate queueing time from compute consistently?

[05:42] Noah Patel: We instrument the scheduler. It’s “time in queue before batch execution.” It’s not perfect in every edge case, but it’s a strong signal. We validate it under load.

[05:55] Mina Sato: And tokens per second, how do you define it? We’ve seen vendors define it differently.

[06:02] Noah Patel: Yeah, fair. We define it as generated tokens divided by decode wall time (and also track end-to-end tokens/sec). We make the definition explicit in the dashboard.

[06:13] Jordan Lee: Okay, so what triggers the baseline? Like do you compare to yesterday? Or a fixed number.

[06:21] Logan Wright: We use versioned baselines. Conceptually: per model + runtime version + hardware SKU, and optionally region. We store baseline distributions derived from controlled load runs. Then the canary compares observed stats against that baseline.

[06:40] Alex Chen: So if your fleet slowly drifts, doesn’t the baseline become stale and alert all the time.

[06:47] Logan Wright: That’s the baseline drift problem, yes. We’re careful about when baselines update. We don’t do a naive rolling baseline that chases the regression.

[06:57] Allison Grant: Operationally, we treat baseline updates like a change. It’s versioned and auditable. And we cap how fast it can move. Plus, the alert logic has dampening.

[07:09] Priya Nair: Could you walk us through an example incident? Like, canary fires, what happens next.

[07:15] Marissa Cole: Sure. Typically incident-bot posts into a channel thread. Warning goes to #eng-runtime. Paging goes to on-call plus runtime.

[07:25] Allison Grant: The alert includes: model, region, observed p95 vs baseline p95, percent change, confidence / sample size, and direct links: dashboard panel, recent deploys, and a runbook.

[07:40] Alex Chen: Does it also include what changed? Like “runtime build id bumped.”

[07:44] Logan Wright: Yes, we attach the runtime build id and kernel tag in the payload. We also link to recent rollout events.

[07:52] Noah Patel: Then triage is usually: 1) confirm it’s not canary noise—check sample size, check if it’s isolated to a region or SKU. 2) check breakdown: queueing vs prefill vs decode. 3) check if kernel tag changed. 4) correlate with fleet changes or release.

[08:10] Jordan Lee: And mitigation?

[08:12] Noah Patel: If it’s a runtime release, we can rollback runtime in that region. If it’s a kernel feature flag, we can flip it off. If it’s capacity, we can adjust scheduling/batching knobs or shift traffic to other pools.

[08:29] Priya Nair: When you say shift traffic, is that on your side or customer side.

[08:33] Marissa Cole: In hosted, it’s on our side. In Dedicated, depends on contract and how isolated the pool is, but we can still do regional fallback if you configure it.

[08:44] Alex Chen: This gets into rollout gates. Do you actually block deployments based on the canary.

[08:50] Marissa Cole: For some classes of runtime and kernel rollouts, yes—we’re moving toward “gates” where sustained canary regression will automatically pause expansion or require explicit override.

[09:04] Jordan Lee: We like that. We’ve had vendors say “we watch dashboards” but that doesn’t stop anything.

[09:09] Allison Grant: To set expectations, we don’t want the gate to flap. So the gate criteria are stricter than warning alerts. It’s like a higher confidence threshold.

[09:20] Priya Nair: What are the actual thresholds, like percent regression.

[09:24] Noah Patel: It depends per model. But as a rough order, you might do something like: warning at +8–10% p95 sustained, page at +15–20% for top models, and similarly for tokens/sec drops, like -10% warning, -20% page. Those are placeholders. We tune based on observed variance.

[09:47] Mina Sato: Do different prompt lengths have different baselines? Because our workload is bimodal.

[09:53] Noah Patel: Great point. We bucket prompt lengths and output lengths. The canary workload is a mixture. We also track those distributions so you can see if the batch is heavy on long prompts that day.

[10:08] Logan Wright: But we’re careful not to explode labels by having per-bucket labels everywhere. Mostly we do histograms and a small number of coarse buckets.

[10:19] Alex Chen: Can you share what “small number” means.

[10:22] Logan Wright: Typically 4–6 prompt length buckets and 3–4 output buckets, but in metrics we store them as histograms rather than as a high-cardinality label set.

[10:35] Priya Nair: Okay, I want to ask about overhead. If this canary is always running, is it stealing GPU capacity from customers.

[10:43] Noah Patel: We have a strict overhead budget. Target is under 0.5% GPU capacity per region for covered models. We enforce QPS caps and a “capacity budget” in the canary service.

[10:58] Jordan Lee: So if you add models, it doesn’t like explode.

[11:01] Noah Patel: Right, there are safeguards to prevent accidental wide fan-out. Config is validated.

[11:07] Alex Chen: Do you run it per region and per GPU type.

[11:11] Noah Patel: Yes, because regressions can be SKU-specific.

[11:15] Priya Nair: That’s important. We’ve seen A100 vs H100 behave differently with the same model.

[11:20] Noah Patel: Exactly.

[11:22] Marissa Cole: Maybe we can pause and ask—what’s your internal SLO policy? Like, do you page on p95, p99.

[11:30] Alex Chen: We page on p95 end-to-end for a few endpoints. p99 is more like a dashboard metric, but it’s the one customers complain about.

[11:39] Jordan Lee: We also care about tokens/sec because it affects user feel in streaming. If it starts slow, people think it’s broken.

[11:47] Noah Patel: That maps well. We track streaming first-token latency too, which often correlates with prefill plus queueing.

[11:56] Mina Sato: First token latency, yes.

[11:59] Logan Wright: Just to clarify, we might call it “time to first token” in the dashboard. Same idea.

[12:05] Priya Nair: Is your SLO expressed as “p95 under X” per model.

[12:10] Allison Grant: Internally we treat it like an SLO-like signal per model, yes. But we also separate “customer-facing SLO” from “regression detection.” The canary is mainly for regressions against baseline, not absolute thresholds.

[12:27] Alex Chen: That’s subtle. So you could have a model that is kind of slow but stable, and it wouldn’t alert.

[12:32] Allison Grant: Correct.

[12:35] Jordan Lee: But for us, we need both. Like absolute SLO and regression.

[12:40] Marissa Cole: Yep. For Dedicated we can align on absolute performance targets and then the canary is like guardrails against changes.

[12:49] Priya Nair: Are canary results visible to customers? Or only internal.

[12:54] Marissa Cole: Today, primarily internal. But we can share relevant dashboard views during an incident, and we’re working on surfacing more in the console over time.

[13:05] Alex Chen: I’d like at least a way to see “is the platform experiencing a regression” without opening a ticket.

[13:10] Marissa Cole: Understood. We can talk about what we can expose during a pilot.

[13:17] Mina Sato: On prompt shapes: do you model tool calling? That can change token patterns.

[13:22] Noah Patel: For the initial canary we keep it mostly plain generation because tool calling is more about the application-level tokens. But we can include structured output parameters, like forcing JSON, because that can change decode behavior a bit.

[13:38] Jordan Lee: Another question: how do you prevent the canary from masking issues by caching? Like if KV cache is hitting.

[13:46] Noah Patel: Good question. We can run both cache-on and cache-off variants, depending on what signal we want. Also we track KV cache hit rate where applicable.

[13:58] Logan Wright: And that hit rate is a metric, not a label. Again cardinality.

[14:03] Alex Chen: You mentioned you had issues with cardinality. What happened.

[14:08] Logan Wright: Early on, people wanted too many dimensions—like model + model version + prompt bucket + customer tier + route + region + SKU… it gets expensive and noisy. We did a cardinality audit and narrowed down the label set.

[14:27] Priya Nair: We’ve had Prometheus bills explode from that.

[14:30] Logan Wright: Yeah exactly.

[14:33] Marissa Cole: We’re at about 15 minutes. Let’s dig into the operational response more since that’s your key concern.

[14:40] Jordan Lee: Yeah. So, pretend it’s Friday night, p95 regresses 20% for the model we’re on. What happens, realistically.

[14:50] Allison Grant: If it’s a top model and it’s sustained with enough samples, it pages on-call. The first check is “is this isolated to one region/SKU.” If yes, mitigation might be to shift traffic away or to drain that pool.

[15:07] Noah Patel: If it correlates with a runtime release, we rollback. We keep rollbacks fast—like minutes—because runtime changes are usually controlled by rollout tooling.

[15:18] Alex Chen: Do you do automatic rollback.

[15:21] Marissa Cole: Not fully automatic across the board yet. We do auto-pause for some. Full auto-rollback is something we’re cautious about because we don’t want to trade one incident for another. But we do have a “halt expansion” mechanism.

[15:38] Jordan Lee: Makes sense.

[15:40] Priya Nair: How do you decide it’s kernel vs scheduler.

[15:45] Noah Patel: The breakdown. If queueing increases, likely scheduler/capacity. If runtime execution per token increases and kernel tag changed, likely kernel. If only prefill changes, it might be model config like rope scaling or context length settings. If only decode slows, could be KV cache behavior or kernel.

[16:08] Mina Sato: Rope scaling? Sorry.

[16:10] Noah Patel: Sorry, RoPE is positional embeddings scaling. Some config changes can affect attention compute.

[16:17] Mina Sato: Got it.

[16:19] Logan Wright: Also the payload includes runtime build id. So you can correlate to a specific release.

[16:26] Alex Chen: Can you show us the payload.

[16:28] Noah Patel: I can’t show in this call because I don’t have it pulled up, but we’ll send a sanitized example. It’s basically: “model=…, region=…, baseline_p95=…, observed_p95=…, delta=…, samples=…,” plus links.

[16:45] Jordan Lee: Links to what.

[16:47] Allison Grant: Grafana panels, trace explorer, and the runbook.

[16:51] Priya Nair: Do you have tracing in the inference path.

[16:54] Logan Wright: We have tracing hooks. For canary traffic, it’s straightforward because it’s tagged as canary. For customer traffic, depends on deployment mode, but we can typically provide correlation ids.

[17:09] Alex Chen: How do you make sure canary traffic is isolated. Like it doesn’t get batched with customer traffic.

[17:15] Noah Patel: It can be configured. In many cases, it shares the same scheduler because we want it to detect the real behavior, including batching heuristics. But we tag it so we can separate metrics. It still consumes minimal QPS.

[17:30] Priya Nair: I can see pros and cons. If it shares scheduler, it sees the real thing. But it could also be impacted by load.

[17:37] Noah Patel: Exactly. That’s why we look at runtime-only vs queueing. If it’s impacted by load, queueing will show it.

[17:48] Jordan Lee: Do you run the canary during peak times.

[17:51] Noah Patel: It’s always on, but rate limited. It gives us coverage across time-of-day effects.

[18:00] Alex Chen: What’s the sampling window.

[18:02] Noah Patel: Depends on model. We tune to get enough samples without exceeding budget. Usually we want at least hundreds of requests per evaluation window for a given model-region-SKU combination.

[18:16] Logan Wright: And if we don’t have enough samples, we don’t alert. That prevents spurious alarms.

[18:22] Mina Sato: For our workloads, we often have long contexts. Do you test long context.

[18:28] Noah Patel: We have long-context buckets, but we may not cover extreme lengths for every model due to cost. For a Dedicated pilot, we can add a “high risk” profile for your pinned model.

[18:44] Priya Nair: That would matter. Our extraction workload can be 8k input tokens.

[18:48] Noah Patel: Yep, then prefill dominates. We should absolutely include that shape.

[18:55] Jordan Lee: Bringing it back to SLA. Do you have a formal latency SLO.
